questions.question: What is the main activity before reading?
questions.correct_answer: Discussing a picture and personal experiences with the teacher and classmates
questions.explanation: To engage students in thinking about their own experiences and connect them to the topic of nature.

questions.question: How did the bird change its perception of the world?
questions.correct_answer: From small, round, and blue to made of straw, leaves, and finally unknown
questions.explanation: The bird's understanding of the world changed as it gained new experiences and observations.

questions.question: What did the bird think the world was made of after its first observation?
questions.explanation: This was the bird's initial impression of the world based on its small, round experience.

questions.question: Why did the bird think the world was made of straw?
questions.correct_answer: Because it lived in a nest made of straw with its mother
questions.explanation: The bird associated straw with its comfortable and safe home environment.

questions.question: What happened to the bird when it came out of its nest for the first time?
questions.explanation: This new experience made the bird realize that its previous understanding was limited.

questions.question: How did the bird describe its second home?
questions.explanation: The bird found a safe and cozy place to live after leaving its first home.

questions.question: What is the main idea of each stanza in the poem?
questions.correct_answer: The bird's changing perception of the world as it gains new experiences
questions.explanation: Each stanza represents a new stage in the bird's understanding of the world.

questions.question: Why did the bird think it had been blind after seeing leaves?
questions.correct_answer: Because it couldn't see beyond its nest
questions.explanation: The bird realized that its limited perspective was not representative of the entire world.

questions.question: What happened to the bird at the end of the poem?
questions.correct_answer: It flew away as an adult, no longer knowing how the world is made
questions.explanation: The bird's journey represents growth and increased understanding, but also the realization that there may be more to learn.

questions.question: What shape was the bird's first house?
questions.explanation: This description reflects the bird's initial limited perspective on the world.

questions.question: How many times did the bird change its home?
questions.explanation: The bird moved from its small, round house to a nest made of straw and then eventually flew away.

notes.title: Bird's Changing Perception
notes.content: The bird's understanding of the world changed as it gained new experiences and observations.

notes.title: Initial Impression of the World
notes.content: The bird initially thought the world was made of pale, blue shell.

notes.title: Straw Nest
notes.content: The bird associated straw with its comfortable and safe home environment.

notes.title: Leaves as a New Perspective
notes.content: The bird realized that its limited perspective was not representative of the entire world after seeing leaves.

notes.title: Growth and Increased Understanding
notes.content: The bird's journey represents growth and increased understanding, but also the realization that there may be more to learn.

notes.title: Bird's Second Home
notes.content: The bird found a safe and cozy place to live after leaving its first home.

notes.title: Poem Structure
notes.content: Each stanza represents a new stage in the bird's understanding of the world.

notes.title: Bird's Final State
notes.content: The bird flew away as an adult, no longer knowing how the world is made.

notes.title: Shape of First House
notes.content: The bird's first house was small, round, and blue.

notes.title: Number of Home Changes
notes.content: The bird changed its home two times.

facts.fact: The poem represents the bird's journey from a limited perspective to increased understanding and growth.
Each stanza in the poem reflects a new stage in the bird's perception of the world.
The bird associated straw with its comfortable home environment.
The bird realized that its limited perspective was not representative of the entire world after seeing leaves.
The poem explores the idea that growth and increased understanding come from experiencing new things.
The bird's final state is one of uncertainty, as it no longer knows how the world is made.
The poem uses the metaphor of a bird's journey to explore human development and perception.
The bird changes its home twice in the course of the poem.
The shape of the bird's first house is described as small, round, and blue.
The bird's experiences are meant to reflect a broader human experience of growing and learning.
